data.title: PSI May Not Fit The Future Of Chip Stocks
data.description:
Let me get straight to the point: in my opinion, the Invesco Semiconductors ETF (NYSEARCA:PSI), which passively tracks an index built on an asset allocation logic different from the classic market weight, will not be able to accurately represent the semiconductor equity segment.

The reason? The sector’s market cap narrowing process is irreversible. I’ll explain more in a moment, but first…

Do you really understand what PSI is?

PSI is a passive ETF that minimizes tracking error by following the Dynamic Semiconductor Intellidex Index, an index that prides itself on having a quantitative stock-picking structure behind it. The selection criteria are based on price momentum, earnings momentum, valuation, quality, and management action.

The result: 30 U.S. semiconductor companies selected from a universe of 2,000 U.S. stocks, using the FactSet Revere Hierarchy. These are split into two groups: large caps (top quintile), with 8 selected stocks that make up 40% of the total weight, and small/mid-caps (bottom 4 quintiles), with 22 stocks making up 60%. Large caps are each given a 5% weight in the index; the others, 2.73%. The index is rebalanced quarterly. And PSI passively follows this structure, with a 0.51% expense ratio.

The result

It’s impossible not to notice that the top three holdings are KLA Corp, Lam Research Corp, and Texas Instruments, companies we’re not exactly used to seeing in the top spots when talking about semiconductor ETFs. What’s more, there are significant omissions, like key international names (e.g., TSM, ASML).

And this is the peculiarity of PSI, something that must be deeply understood. The benchmark essentially applies a factor in a passive way, which has a disruptive impact on the fund’s asset allocation.

You need to know that

Since PSI follows pre-defined construction models, the first question that comes to mind is: how does it perform compared to a market-weighted ETF? My picks are SMH and SOXX. And the overall result is that PSI is, on average, underperforming. Or more precisely:

  • Excluding the last 5 years, PSI actually delivered very competitive returns, even compared to SMH, which is considered the top performer in the category. So, a backward-looking analysis done in 2020 would have likely labeled PSI as at least a “competitive” ETF.

  • But since 2020, it started to underperform, resulting in a 10-year underperformance compared to SMH.

The reason? With market cap becoming increasingly concentrated in fewer names, market-weighted ETFs have simply done a better job at representing the sector.

To avoid the same mistake we could’ve made in 2020, I now ask: from a forward-looking perspective, which market cap structure will best represent the sector going forward? I have reason to believe that PSI may continue to underperform, even looking ahead.

The real reason behind the underperformance

According to a McKinsey report, which I consider a must-read for anyone looking to ride the semiconductor wave, only 5% of companies generated nearly all the sector’s economic profit in 2024. Among them, of course: Nvidia, TSMC, Broadcom, and ASML.

And I ask myself: how can an ETF that assigns predefined weights to companies truly represent such a concentrated market? The answer? It simply can’t.

Let’s be clear: this doesn’t mean PSI will deliver negative returns. The sector shows a 19% ROE, and revenue is expected to grow at an average annual rate of 10% through 2030. But if you break down where that growth is coming from, it’s always the same winners, and PSI is unlikely to emphasize them.

AI-focused companies are projected to grow revenues by 18%, especially if U.S.-China relations remain stable. On the other hand, non-AI-focused companies, or those not tied to China, are expected to grow just 2–3%. That’s not bad, but it’s a completely different outlook.

And in a market this imbalanced, I believe market-weighted solutions do a better job representing the true dynamics.

Risk

On the other hand, market-weighted ETFs are going through an unusual phase: SMH has nearly 20% in NVDA, making it one of the most concentrated ETFs in the sector.

That is a risk, in the sense that, yes, the market is heavily concentrated in a few tickers, but that also increases concentration risk, no matter how strong today’s growth prospects may be.

We all know the future isn’t written, especially in a time of major political uncertainty like the one we’re in now.

Conclusion

To conclude, I believe the right rating for PSI is SELL.

It’s not a SELL on the semiconductor sector itself, it’s a SELL on an ETF whose construction, in other contexts, I would have considered rewarding.

But in a vertical and highly concentrated market like semiconductors, its structure feels too diluted, and in my view, it’s likely to deliver less alpha than its peers.

data.title: British American Tobacco: Time To Take Profits?
data.description:
British American Tobacco (NYSE:BTI) (OTCPK:BTAFF) is one of the world’s largest tobacco companies, operating in over 180 countries. Although historically a combustible cigarette business, the company is increasingly diversifying its revenue mix into next generational products. These include vaping devices, heat-not-burn devices, and modern oral (a tobacco-free nicotine pouch). The US is the Group’s most important market and accounts for 45% of its revenue. The balance is made up of some developed markets, but predominantly consists of emerging markets. Despite having appreciated about 60% YoY in price and trading at a higher multiple compared to its historical average, the company is making smart investments in growing product categories and divesting from laggards, all against a favorable regulatory backdrop. For me, it's a Buy.

Why It's Pricier

British American Tobacco currently trades on a P/E of 10X. This reflects a material re-rating from the sub 6X P/E multiple an investor could buy the share on just two years ago. There have been stock-specific as well as macro-related factors driving this re-rating. From an operational perspective, management has been successful in de-gearing the balance sheet, with net debt to EBITDA now at 2.4X, having peaked above 4X in 2017. This has been driven by continued excellent cash generation, as well as the sale of a stake in their ITC associate business in India.

Moreover, management has demonstrated progress in Next Generational Products, with the category moving into profitable territory. A segment like Modern Oral, although small, is performing tremendously well and can move the needle on Group earnings when earnings are only growing in the low single digits.

On the macro front, a global flight to safety has resulted in tobacco stocks generally re-rating, and BATS has benefitted from this trend.

Perhaps most importantly, the company has continued to demonstrate exceptional cash generation ability. While there have been doubts in the past over the company’s ability to maintain the progressive dividend policy, these doubts have essentially been put to bed, thanks to strong operational cash flows, balance sheet de-gearing, and management’s willingness to monetize the ITC stake.

Although the dividend growth has slowed, it has remained progressive. The 10-year dividend per share compound annual growth rate is 5% (In GBP), and this falls to 3.3% over the past 3 years. Nevertheless, there was more than enough margin of safety in the valuation historically such that any dividend growth was not priced into the share. Current free cash flow per share of GBP3.5 implies a free cash flow yield of 10%. With cash generation expected to remain robust and a key feature of the investment case, this will be more than sufficient to maintain a progressive dividend (Current dividend yield is 7%), with some cash left over for buybacks and deleveraging.

Valuation Looking Full?

Given management guidance for low single digit earnings growth this year, moving to mid-single digit growth next year, a 10X P/E could be deemed a fair rating for the stock. This is particularly probably considering the myriad regulatory issues the company continues to face as well as the issues being faced in U.S. vaping, with illicit Chinese disposables gaining 70% share of the market.

A back of the envelope returns calculation would imply ~12% returns p.a., given a 7% dividend yield and assuming 5% growth in earnings per year, and no further re-rating in the stock’s multiple. This is reasonable, but not terribly exciting. However, I would argue investors should extend their horizon by a few years and consider British American Tobacco’s trajectory in 3–5 years’ time. There are the beginnings of some interesting developments which may boost BAT’s earnings trajectory in time.

Groundwork for More Growth

First, the Chinese illicit disposables issue. With illegal disposable vaping devices having garnered 70% of the market in the US, British American Tobacco stands to gain enormously as the number one legal player once the tide starts to turn against illegal disposables. This is already in the early stages of development. President Trump recently made several key appointments, including:

  • Robert Kennedy as Secretary for US Health and Human Services.
  • Marty Makary as FDA Commissioner.
  • Bret Koplow as acting CTP (Center for Tobacco Products).

Importantly, these positions were recently occupied by individuals that had demonstrated distrust of tobacco. Robert Kennedy has made several statements voicing support for the fight against illegal disposables. He has also been seen using modern oral products. His conduct thus far implies a more friendly demeanor towards British American Tobacco's products, tobacco and non-tobacco alike.

An executive order from the President banning disposable vapes is also a possibility, and would likely be the fastest and most effective measure in reducing illegal disposables in the market materially. Otherwise, increased inter-agency cooperation, more media attention and state by state regulation. Louisiana, for example, enacted legislation in 2023 requiring all manufacturers to register their products – this proved highly effective in combatting illicit disposables. Ten states have since followed suit. These are all the kinds of measures that will start to turn the tide against illicit disposables.

British American Tobacco generated £1.8 billion in revenue from vaping in 2025. Of this, £1 billion was from the U.S. This is 4% of Group revenue, and this is a crucial component of the revenue mix given positive high growth rates for the category, versus negative growth rates for U.S. combustibles. With value share of the legal market at 50%, this implies British American Tobacco has 15% share of the total market, including illicit. This means if the illegal market halved in share and British American Tobacco gained proportionally, market share could increase from 15% of the total market to 33%, thus almost a doubling of revenue from this category in the U.S. That’s 4 percentage points of additional revenue growth. Given management is only guiding for low single digit revenue growth, the boost could be enormously beneficial to earnings.

Modern Oral

Another potential growth catalyst is the Modern Oral category. Although small in the current mix, at approximately 3% of Group revenue, the growth rates here are impressive and potentially needle-moving. In FY 2024, its Modern Oral revenues were up 53% in constant currency organic terms. The 4.5-year CAGR since H1 2020 has been 48%. If this growth rate can be sustained, this category can add 1–2 percentage points to Group revenue per year in the next few years, and more as it increases in relative size. Their Velo 2.0 Modern Oral product has proved successful outside of the U.S. (leadership position in 21 European markets), and with a pending Premarket Tobacco Market Application (PMTA) approval in the U.S., British American Tobacco is poised to replicate its success in the U.S. market. Philip Morris' Zyn still dominates the US market; however, BAT is well positioned with Velo 2.0, which is a much improved product over the first iteration, to challenge the incumbent once PMTA approval is received.

Competition in this space is intense, however. In the US, the market is gate-kept by the FDA through the PMTA approval process. Philip Morris’ Zyn modern oral product accounts for 68% market share, with Altria’s On! Product in second position at 19% share. British American Tobacco’s Velo product currently only has 6% share. However, this is the first generation product and is widely regarded as vastly inferior to the likes of Zyn. Therein lies the opportunity once Velo 2.0 receives PMTA approval.

Valuation

My valuation methodology for British American Tobacco has historically been focused on dividends, as this has accounted for the bulk of investor returns for the stock. I expect re-rating to play a more important part in returns in the next few years, given the above catalysts mentioned. Using a 3-year dividend discount model with an exit P/E multiple of 12.4x in year 3, the stock is currently 15% undervalued. Bear in mind that historically, the company used to trade on a mid-teens multiple. With the business mix evolving for the better over the medium term into higher-growth categories, there is potential for British American Tobacco to begin to close the rating gap to a Philip Morris, for example, which trades on a PE of 23x. The risk is to the upside in my view.

Risks

Risks to watch out for which would necessitate changing this view include failure to secure PMTA approval in the US for Velo 2.0, as well as ineffective regulation or measures implemented against illegal Chinese disposables. Chinese exporters are likely to try to circumvent any measures, potentially round-tripping through other countries. Additionally, an eye should most certainly be kept on the US combustibles market, where volumes are falling 9% p.a. A further deterioration, which, I think, is unlikely, would impact the company’s cash flows. This remains an important component of British American Tobacco's earnings.

Final Thoughts

Overall, I think investors need to be careful when simply assuming management guidance for this year and next and extrapolating into the future. Management has missed guidance in the past to the downside, backtracking on their high single-digit earnings growth, and then mid-single-digit earnings growth guidance, to where we find ourselves today: low single-digit earnings growth. I would suggest it is possible the company may revise their guidance numbers upwards in time, given the potential catalysts highlighted above. This is likely a few years out, however. With the market being forward-looking, it would be wise not to try time when the potential good news is priced in. At the same time, the dividend yield remains attractive at 7%. I would be hesitant to take profits too quickly on British American Tobacco.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Oaktree Specialty Lending: Credit Issues Still A Problem
data.description:
Investors in Oaktree Specialty Lending Corporation (NASDAQ:OCSL) are still paying the price for an above average non-accrual ratio, as the BDC’s stock continues to sell at an above-average discount to net asset value.

The business development company, however, reset its dividend in the prior quarter and changed its dividend composition, which may lead to relief for the coverage ratio moving forward.

The new dividend consists of a base dividend, that has been realigned with the BDC’s lowered net investment income, and Oaktree Specialty Lending is going to pay supplemental dividends that correspond to its ability to produce excess net investment income (above what is needed to fund the base dividend).

High investment losses and higher than normal non-accruals in the credit portfolio forced the business development company to lower its dividend in 1Q25.

My last stock classification on Oaktree Specialty Lending was ‘Hold’ which I have to reaffirm since the BDC’s non-accruals got even worse in the March-quarter. Though the dividend pay-out trend might improve moving forward, the non-accrual issue is probably going to remain a drag on the BDC’s net investment income and NAV multiple.

Troubled Credit Portfolio Weighed Down By High Non-Accruals

Oaktree Specialty Lending is a specialty financing company that provides its portfolio companies with access to much-needed capital and that can’t easily access financing from risk-averse traditional banks.

As of March 31, 2025, the business development company had a total investment portfolio valued at $2.9 billion, with about 84 percent of all investments being allocated to First and Second Liens.

The core investment focus of Oaktree Specialty Lending is on the software and service business sector, which is characterized by highly predictable sales and income.

While Oaktree Specialty Lending did originate new investments in the last twelve months, the business development company unfortunately saw substantial net asset value pressure related to bad investments it made in the past.

In the last twelve months, Oaktree Specialty Lending reported cumulative total net realized and unrealized losses in the amount of $164.9 million and the business development company suffered losses in every single quarter in the last year, as shown by the yellow bar in the next chart.

The underlying problem for the business development company is credit quality which has taken a bad hit in the last year and, thus, Oaktree Specialty Lending’s non-accrual trend is not looking great, to put it mildly: The BDC is exhibiting a substantial deterioration in its credit quality over the last twelve months which caused the non-accrual ratio to tick up to 4.6% as of March 31, 2025, reflecting a YoY increase of 2.2 percentage point.

Non-accruals rose 0.7 percentage points QoQ and 10, instead of 9, companies were not performing.

Oaktree Specialty Lending’s non-accrual trend looks ugly:

Dividend Presently Fully Covered

The business development company is presently dealing with dividend pay-out metrics that are not great, but are getting better. Oaktree Specialty Lending earned $0.45 per share in adjusted net investment income in the March-quarter, which reflected a substantial 20% YoY decrease in income.

Because Oaktree Specialty Lending adjusted its dividend lower in the prior quarter, however, the business development company managed to cover its full dividend pay-out of $0.40 per share with net investment income.

For 2Q25, Oaktree Specialty Lending declared its base dividend of $0.40 per share, which is going to get supplemented by an extra payment of $0.02 per share as a means to distribute excess portfolio income. Both dividends are payable on June 30, 2025.

In terms of net investment income, a widely underperforming credit portfolio has obviously had a negative impact on Oaktree Specialty Lending’s results in 2Q25. The BDC suffered a YoY drop in its adjusted total investment income of 21% in the March-quarter to $77.2 million as a larger number of loans stopped producing income. This decrease in income has been anticipated by the business development company and has led to a 27% dividend cut.

Thanks to this dividend realignment, Oaktree Specialty Lending is more likely than not going to see healthier dividend pay-out metrics moving forward.

While OCSL might see a higher margin of safety as far as the dividend is concerned, the non-accrual situation is not yet resolved, which is why my stock classification for Oaktree Specialty Lending remains 'Hold.'

Discount To BV Appropriate Given High Non-Accruals

Oaktree Specialty Lending is obviously going through a rough patch right now, and passive income investors have paid the price here as well: The stock of Oaktree Specialty Lending has dramatically underperformed in the last year and lost about 26% of its value. As a consequence, the non-accrual issue has resulted in a distressed valuation or Oaktree Specialty Lending, which is likely appropriate given its much higher than normal non-accruals.

At the time of writing, the stock of Oaktree Specialty Lending is selling for a 13% discount to net asset value. Oaktree Specialty Lending is not the only business development company with a sub-optimal non-accrual ratio.

There are a couple of other business development companies that I have covered, such as TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC (TPVG), Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD) and FS KKR Capital Corp (FSK) that all have higher than average non-accrual ratios and thus also sell at substantial discounts to net asset value.

In terms of Price-To-NII ratio, Oaktree Specialty Lending is selling for a valuation multiple 8% above the BDC peer group average ratio of 7.5x. Business development companies with weak credit profiles tend to sell for either big discounts to NAV or low Price-To-NII ratios, which is both the case here for Oaktree Specialty Lending as well as other BDCs working through their credit issues.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Not Be Bullish

The investment thesis for Oaktree Specialty Lending can turn bullish if Oaktree Specialty Lending gravitates toward healthier dividend pay-out metrics while keeping a lid on its non-accrual ratio.

Only in the event that the business development company can restore healthier credit quality will a re-rating to net asset value be possible. Anything short of it will most probably lead to a continued depressed valuation multiple for the business development company.

My Takeaway

Oaktree Specialty Lending is going through some serious pain and portfolio realignment issues which have been characterized by an increase in investment losses, both unrealized and realized, and an unfavorable non-accrual trend in its credit portfolio. That being said, I think that the pay-out trend is going to show promise with the dividend realignment and the discount to net asset value widely signals that investors understand the BDC’s credit issues.

Since the dividend has been restructured and realigned with lower net investment income expectations, Oaktree Specialty Lending should be able to see a rising margin of safety moving forward.

Only if OCSL also improves its non-accruals are we going to see a stock re-rating, in my view. And since the BDC’s non-accruals continued to rise in the last quarter, it may be quite a while until this happens. Hold.

data.title: This Is The Price I'd Buy Shopify
data.description:
Shopify (NASDAQ:SHOP) (TSX:SHOP:CA) is appearing to be a clear beneficiary of generative AI. The e-commerce landscape remains somewhat fragmented even as the story matures, but SHOP is shaping up to be a potential long-term consolidator as it has offered an intentionally completed product portfolio. The company maintains a net cash balance sheet and is solidly GAAP profitable. I expect the company to sustain aggressive top- and bottom-line growth for many years, but also acknowledge that the current valuation appears to already have priced this in. SHOP remains one of the highest quality names in my coverage, but the math just doesn’t work out here. This isn’t the time to chase returns or fall victim to the “fear of missing out.” Wait for better prices or look for cheaper alternatives elsewhere - I rate the stock with a neutral rating.

SHOP Stock Price

I last covered SHOP in March, where I recommended avoiding the stock due to the lofty valuation. The stock has declined by 1% since then, underperforming the broader market by around 5%.

The business is performing exemplarily, but the math is still difficult, as I worry that upgrading the stock here might simply be bull market capitulation.

SHOP Stock Key Metrics

Shopify is the most important enabler of e-commerce when retailers are not looking to support the Amazon (AMZN) death star. Kidding aside, SHOP does offer a fully comprehensive product suite to help retailers with their online businesses.

SHOP has been able to take so much market share in part due to its decision to outsource or partner for much of its functionality, enabling it to offer a more comprehensive product suite even if it is not owning the full profit picture.

The latest quarter saw the company deliver 27% YoY revenue growth to $2.4 billion, exceeding guidance for “mid-twenties” growth.

It is astounding that the company has continued to sustain such aggressive GMV and revenue growth, even as it already posted 20% or higher growth in 2023 and 2024.

The company has been able to sustain such rapid growth due to its strong execution as well the continued growth of e-commerce, with international being the largest growth opportunity.

The company remained highly committed to profitable growth. While gross margins deteriorated 190 bps, operating margins jumped 400 bps as operating leverage took hold. I expect margins to rise even more moving forward, as management appears to have struck a nice balance between top and bottom-line growth.

SHOP ended the quarter with $5.5 billion of cash versus $919 million of debt, representing a strong net cash position. In comparison with just 3 years ago during the pandemic, SHOP has transformed itself into a high-margin machine.

Looking ahead, management has guided for the second quarter to show another round of mid-twenties growth.

On the conference call, management tried to frame the company as a potential beneficiary of the tariff escalations, noting that they have rolled out products such as “duty inclusive pricing” and “tariffguide.ai” to help retailers navigate through the uncertainty. While I do not expect the company to earn material revenues, if any, from these kinds of products, they are examples of how the company continues to differentiate itself from the competition through innovation.

Management used to have a reputation for having a “growth at any cost mindset” but those days are long gone. Management noted that they have a new policy in which teams cannot request additional headcount without first submitting a report showing that they could not have met “their goals using AI-first.” Management noted that the company has shown U.S. e-commerce GMV growth roughly double overall U.S. e-commerce growth for each of the past five quarters, highlighting both the strength of its customer base as well as how its platform has helped its customers. Finally, management noted that the end of the de minimis exemption is not likely to have a meaningful impact as “only 1% of our overall GMV is related to imports from China.”

Is SHOP Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, SHOP traded just shy of 13x sales. At first glance, that might look like a bargain relative to tech peers, given consensus estimates for 18% to 20% growth for the next decade.

However, we must remember that gross margins stand at right around 50% here. Given that the cost of revenues is primarily payment processing fees, I see little reason to believe that gross margins can inflect higher from here. I expect the company to generate 25% net margins over the long term. This is higher than consensus estimates for 17% net margins by 2033. That places the current valuation at around 51x long-term earnings. If we assume that the company can sustain 18% to 20% growth through 2033, then that places the stock at around 12x 2033 long-term earnings. That suggests around 9% annual return potential over the next 8.5 years. While that would not be a terrible result, I’d argue that it is not offering enough of a premium relative to the market given the aggressive assumption regarding top-line growth. Perhaps some investors might think that the 25% net margin assumption is too conservative. I’d push back on this, as 25% net margins would imply 50% net margins based on gross profits. We can see below that the company’s incremental margins were 55% based on gross profits. That makes it appear very unlikely for long-term net margins to rise above 50% based on gross profits.

SHOP is a high-quality name and thus might deserve a lower hurdle rate, but even then, I’d need around 12% projected annual returns to get interested here. That would represent a stock price of around $85 per share.

SHOP Stock Conclusion

SHOP is looking like a consolidator in a still-growing e-commerce market. Management has found a way to balance aggressive top-line growth with expanding GAAP profitability. The company maintains a net cash balance sheet and the cost discipline is showing in the numbers. Consensus estimates are quite optimistic for the company, but the stock is priced such that the reward does not look appealing even if it meets those high expectations. I recommend investors look for cheaper alternatives among high-quality growth names, as I have done at Best of Breed Growth Stocks. I rate the stock a neutral rating.

data.title: Arbe Robotics: I See The Vision, But Not The Business
data.description:
Thesis

Arbe Robotics (NASDAQ:ARBE), born out of Tel Aviv, is building ultra-high-resolution 4D imaging radar, the kind of tech that’s supposed to make autonomous vehicles see the world with surgical precision. The company positions itself right at the heart of the software-defined vehicle movement, talking up sensor fusion and next-gen driver assistance like it’s already there. My research argues that Arbe is caught between its own ambition and the fact that it’s operating in a region under constant geopolitical threat. You can see the outlines of something important like real tech and real partnerships, but there’s still no proof that this turns into a real business.

The State of Arbe Robotics

This week, Israel launched airstrikes against Iranian nuclear sites, prompting direct missile retaliation on Israeli cities, including Tel Aviv, Arbe's headquarters. If a company’s engineers don’t feel safe coming to work, or can’t access the lab, innovation stalls and operational continuity, things like access to suppliers, product testing timelines, and talent retention, gets thrown into question when you're in an active conflict zone.

Analyst's note: Yes, I know the company has a Texas office, but when you look at where they’re actually trying to grow, it’s all happening in Tel Aviv. The new hiring: product managers, engineers, dev ops, safety roles, is concentrated there. That’s where the expansion is, and that’s where the center of gravity still is.

Arbe, being a micro-cap stock, already faces high volatility and light liquidity. The added layer of regional instability means many investors might now add a higher risk premium on it, demanding more upside before they even think about buying. That’s a problem, especially when you learn that just a week before the conflict escalated, on June 5, Arbe filed to raise up to $50 million via a mixed shelf offering. While normally that could be read as a forward-looking move to fund growth, under current conditions it might be interpreted as a lifeline, one that you might hesitate to grab unless there’s either a de-escalation in the region or undeniable signs of commercial momentum.

Yet while the risks are very real, so too are the tailwinds pushing Arbe forward. Zooming out from the Middle East, the market for automotive radar is undeniably growing. From $6.66 billion in 2025 to a projected $22.94 billion in 2035, with a CAGR of 13.2%, radar is going mainstream. What used to be high-end features like adaptive cruise control or blind spot detection are now baseline expectations, even in mid-tier cars. And Arbe's pushing the envelope with 4D imaging radar that can see and interpret a vehicle’s surroundings in real time, even through fog or heavy rain.

And that kind of tech matters more than ever because the broader auto industry is in transition, whether it’s fully autonomous driving or more advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS), every automaker is looking for smarter eyes on the road. Arbe is caught squarely in the churn of a structural shift because radar technology is finally getting cheap enough and simple enough to plug into the mainstream auto supply chain without much fuss. Regulators are doing their part to grease the wheels: in North America, safety mandates are picking up steam; in Europe, they've already been marching under banners like Vision Zero; and in Asia-Pacific, where China’s top-down planning makes things move fast, governments are throwing serious weight behind smart mobility infrastructure. Radar is becoming standard.

Even so, competition is fierce, and the road to mass production is long. Arbe has two key bets. One is sensor fusion, combining radar with cameras and LiDAR, which many in the industry now see as the best path forward. The other is software-defined radar, which can adapt via over-the-air updates. I think this second piece is especially strategic, offering automakers long-term cost savings and technical flexibility, though it’s still emerging and carries execution risk.

To be very clear, this is still a company in early innings. Technical hurdles like interference in crowded radar bands (24 GHz and 77 GHz), and integration costs that can limit adoption in lower-end vehicles, are very real. But Arbe has shown they’re not standing still. In Q1, they hit key milestones with Tier 1 suppliers and OEMs, especially in Europe. Their radar tech, already embedded in Magna’s platforms, is being tested by big auto brands. CEO Kobi Marenko said sourcing decisions could be finalized soon, potentially locking Arbe into multi-year production contracts that start by 2028.

In China, things are moving even faster. Arbe’s long-range radar system, the LRR615, is on track for mass production in Q4 2025, thanks to their partnership with HiRain. Marenko even suggested that commercialization in China could outpace North America by a full year. That’s important because faster commercialization means faster revenue realization, something, I'm willing to guess, you crave in a cash-burning growth story like this one.

Moreover, Arbe isn’t putting all its eggs in the automotive basket. They just landed an order from Sensrad for over 1,000 radar chips to be used in areas like smart infrastructure and factory automation, the kind of diversification that could help buffer against any automotive sector slowdown or supply-chain issues.

On the tech front, Arbe's radar clocks in at 20 frames per second with on-chip super-resolution and those are solid specs which got the nod at CES 2025 and made the rounds on the global demo circuit. Partnerships with NVIDIA (NVDA) and Horizon Robotics don’t hurt either, making their system easier to bolt onto whatever software stack the automakers are already married to. And critically, the most recent numbers reveal that Arbe also raised $33 million in a direct offering, followed by $21.5 million from bond conversions, bringing cash reserves to $71.9 million. With expected cash burn of $32–34 million a year, they’ve got a two-year runway. That’s enough to keep R&D humming and support global expansion while they chase commercial scale. Gross margins are expected to be 30–35% at launch, with room to grow to 50–60% as production scales.

Assessing Arbe Robotics: Risks, Growth Path, and Valuation Disconnect

That noted, the company’s present reality is marked by an alarming absence of (significant) revenue, mounting losses, and a timeline that seems to be stretching ever further into the future.

To begin with, the company’s revenue fell off a cliff, down 70.8% year over year to just $40,000 for the quarter. That number is so low it barely registers on the scale for a tech company claiming to have breakthrough automotive hardware. And the full-year outlook of $2 million to $5 million, already quite meager, is back-end loaded, which basically means they’re expecting most of that money will show up later in the year. But that hinges on a series of steps like component delivery, OEM approvals, manufacturing readiness, all of which are slow, complicated, and highly vulnerable to delay. And unfortunately, as of now, Arbe hasn’t yet shown it can consistently convert its partnerships into real sales.

At the same time, costs continue to pile up. Operating expenses rose to $13.1 million this quarter from $12.5 million last year, and full-year projections land between $32 million and $34 million. That wouldn’t necessarily be troubling for a pre-revenue tech firm, if not for the fact that Arbe continues to post gross profits in the negative: -$300,000 this quarter, exactly where it was a year ago.

This lack of scalability is troubling because it shows the company isn’t inching closer to profitability, even on the unit level, while the gross margin over the past year stands at -121.76%, whereas the industry median is over +50%.

Now, geographically speaking, aside from this new war, things aren’t much better. In Europe, volume production through that aforementioned Tier 1 supplier Magna is three years away. In the U.S., thanks to disarray in the autonomous vehicle sector (like GM dismantling Cruise and Ford backing out of Argo AI), meaningful revenue may not show up until late 2028, tied to 2029 model-year vehicles. And while China looks slightly more promising with production via HiRain projected for late 2025, even that date is tentative. Management has already signaled that delays are on the table, with timelines slipping into 2026 if, say, customer demand stalls or infrastructure doesn’t show up on time.

What emerges here is a pattern: multiple regional efforts are all technically in progress, but none are close to producing meaningful revenue anytime soon.

This reality poses serious problems when you consider that Arbe’s cash reserves are dwindling, only $0.06 per share in cash remains. Cash flow is negative, and operating cash flow dropped by more than $30 million year-over-year. Even more striking is that capital expenditures account for 84.35% of revenue over the past 12 months, meaning almost every dollar that comes in is immediately reinvested because the company is trying to stay alive long enough to realize its vision.

Despite all this, Wall Street remains very optimistic and Arbe’s market cap reflects sky-high expectations, with valuation multiples that are, frankly, just too extreme.

An illustration of this, the EV/Sales ratio is 203.12 on a trailing basis and 46.97 forward. The Price/Sales ratios are equally outsized: 202.68 TTM and 55.81 FWD. For comparison, most companies in this sector trade at P/S ratios closer to 3. The implication here is that investors are pricing in massive, imminent growth that, to be candid, has shown no signs of materializing.

The gap between hype and reality shows up in the earnings projections too. Analysts are calling for revenue growth of 110% in 2025, then, in terms of EPS estimates, slamming the brakes down to 6.15% in 2026, before bouncing back to 18.03% and 76% in 2027 and 2028, the kind of volatility that suggests the forecasts may be hanging on the outcome of a few big deals rather than broad-based traction. But without detailed explanations for what will drive those earnings, like gross margin improvement, software monetization, or successful design-ins converting into signed contracts, these estimates look to me like speculative modeling rather than grounded forecasts.

In other words, if I were to sum it all up: Arbe is still a company running on promise. And that promise is getting more expensive by the quarter. Investors hoping for a turnaround are effectively making a bet, not an investment in the traditional sense, that multiple things will go right, across multiple regions, all within tight timeframes. But the past year, and unfortunately, this new war, suggests otherwise: revenue down 46.41% even as the sector grew 6%, and no meaningful traction to show for years of investment.

Final Verdict: Sell

Look, I get the long-term pitch on Arbe, the tech sounds impressive, but right now, what you’ve got is a company with almost no revenue, long delays in getting anything to market, and valuation multiples that seem to have detached from gravity. I can’t get around the fact that this is a cash-burning outfit, still pre-revenue, operating out of a war zone, trying to raise money, and with no concrete evidence that any of its partnerships will actually turn into real, scaled-up business.

data.title: PMTW: A 9% Senior Note IPO From PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

This short analysis is the next installment in our series of informative articles dedicated to an exchange-traded fixed-income security IPO. In February 2025, we covered the PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust's (NYSE:PMT) senior notes (PMTV), and now it is time for us to turn our attention to the company's newly issued 9.00% Senior Notes due 6/15/2030 (NYSE:PMTW). As usual in this kind of situation, we will put the new issue through our fixed-income IPO evaluation procedure to find out if it can be a valuable addition to our portfolio.

The New Issue

The prospectus contains all relevant information regarding the new baby bond. Here, we will highlight only the metrics that are most important to us and use them in the following analysis.

PMTW pays 9% annual interest in $0.5625 quarterly distributions. PennyMac Mortgage's gross proceeds from the new security are $100 million for a total of 4 million notes issued with a par value of $25 each, so the issue is by $50 million smaller than PMTV four months ago. PMTW was not rated by any of the three big credit rating agencies at the date of its IPO. It is a mid-term obligation of PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust with a maturity date of 6/15/2030 and is callable on and after 6/15/2027.

As the issue is just listed, its price chart looks like this:

Currently, PMTW is trading at a $24.81 price with a Yield to Maturity of 9.51%, and the Yield To Call is calculated to be 9.75%. This is what the stock's YTC curve looks like right now:

The Company

Founded in 2009 and headquartered in Westlake Village, California, PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust is a specialty finance company that is externally managed by PNMAC Capital Management, LLC, an indirect subsidiary of PennyMac Financial Services, Inc. The Trust invests primarily in residential mortgage loans and mortgage-related assets, and has elected to be taxed as a real estate investment trust (REIT) for U.S. federal income tax purposes. PennyMac Mortgage conducts its line of operations through its Credit Sensitive Strategies, Interest Rate Sensitive Strategies, and Correspondent Production segments.

Below are some numbers to give context to the description of each of the PennyMac segments in the First Quarter of 2025. All the segments of the company are underperforming their results from the last Quarter of 2024, as Interest Rate Sensitive Strategies were operating at a loss, and the net result for the common shareholders is a loss too:

A detailed look at market-driven values shows that the appreciation in Agency MBS was not enough to cover the decline in the Mortgage Servicing Rights and interest rate hedges.

A more detailed observation of the quarter results shows that the market-driven decline in the net fair value of the agency and non-agency senior MBS and interest rate hedges could not be fully offset by the fair value appreciation of the Mortgage Servicing Rights:

When we were looking at the Q4'24 result, we observed the following:

Two quarters are not a long observation period, but it appears to me that it might not be the best hedge possible, as the company is losing money in both directions.

As we observed a couple of months ago, the debt maturity schedule of PennyMac was rather decent, and nothing has changed since. The different types of debt are coming to maturity in manageable portions for the next couple of years. PMTV and now PMTW are creating a portion of approximately $270 million of senior unsecured debt maturing in 2030, and this is quite in line with the solid debt schedule PennyMac is having so far:

Company Performance

The daily chart below shows how the market evaluates the common stock PMT:

We'll compare the total return performance for the past ten years of PMT to that of the general market, represented by the S&P 500, and to the average mortgage REIT, represented by iShares Mortgage Real Estate Capped ETF (REM):

PMT has consistently outperformed the sector in total return since 2018, managing to recover from the COVID crisis faster and to a greater extent. It has even outperformed the general market for quite some time, but since 2021, it's lagging behind it. As for the future expectations for the company, both SA and Wall Street analysts are predominantly neutral on the company, scoring it with a "Hold" rating:

Capital Structure

Below is the capital structure of PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust as of the First Quarter of 2025. The capital structure for the previous four quarters is also presented for the sake of comparison in the short term.

As of Q1'25, PennyMac Mortgage had a total debt of $12.7 billion. With the newly issued notes, the company's total debt increased to $12.8 billion, ranking senior to the $542 million in preferred equity. The unsecured senior notes of the company as of March 31, 2025, were worth $773 million, and the new issue PMTW will increase them by $100 million to a total value of $873 million. However, all the secured debt of PennyMac Mortgage is considered senior to the unsecured notes, as can be seen from the PMTW's prospectus:

As of March 31, 2025, we had total consolidated indebtedness to third parties (excluding trade payables and other liabilities) of $12.7 billion, $11.9 billion of which was secured indebtedness to which the notes would have ranked effectively junior and $11.1 billion of which was indebtedness of our subsidiaries that do not guarantee the notes to third parties to which the notes would have been structurally subordinated.

A particular problem for the senior unsecured debt holders in the case of liquidation could be the approximately $3.5 billion of secured long-term recourse debt, the company has outstanding:

At the bottom of the capital structure is the common equity of $1.4 billion, which is quite insufficient to cover the whole indebtedness of the company. In the case of liquidation, the secured recourse debt will have the first claim on the equity in the amount by which the collateral is insufficient.

Dividends

PennyMac Mortgage has a history of 14 consecutive years without dividend suspension, but its last dividend cut of 15% was in December 2022 and since then, there has been no dividend increase. This is what the company's dividend history looks like:

The company is paying its common shareholders a $0.40 quarterly distribution for a total of a $1.60 TTM dividend. With the current market price of PMT of $12.88, that corresponds to a dividend yield of 12.42%. This is how the dividend yield looks for the past ten years:

The forward estimations for the common stock distribution and dividend yield look like this:

PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust has approximately 86.9 million common shares outstanding, and with a $1.60 forward estimated dividend, it amounts to around $139 million in common share distributions for the next four quarters. The preferred stock payment amounts to $42.3 million annually. The total interest expenses for the past four quarters, according to the income statements of the company, were $725.3 million. PMTW payments will increase the interest expenses by $9 million annually to a total of $734.3 million in debt interest. That is around 4 times the sum of the common and preferred equity's annual dividends. The equity shares' annual distributions represent a small buffer for the company's interest payments, being able to cover only a quarter's worth of interest payments. The creditors of PennyMac should monitor closely the company's financial state.

PMTW credit quality assessment

PMTW was issued with a 9.00% nominal yield, or approximately 500 basis points above the 30-year treasury yields at the time of the IPO.

Despite not being scored by any of the major credit rating agencies, we can compare the PMTW's IPO credit spread to that of the average Single-B rated US corporate debt and see that, according to the underwriters, the security is priced somewhere below Single-B in credit quality. This is an assessment that is putting the new security among the vehicles that carry a substantial investment risk, as shown in the table below:

PennyMac Mortgage exchange-traded issues

Besides the new IPO PMTW, PennyMac Mortgage has two more senior unsecured notes outstanding - PMTV and PMTU. These are the perfect comparison candidates for PMTW, as they are the same type of issues as the new one:

Looking at the risk premiums of the senior debt issues, we see a trend that does not speak in favor of PennyMac Mortgage - the IPO credit spreads are continuously increasing. Even PMTV, which was issued four months ago, was valued by the underwriters with by 30 basis points lower risk premium. The new issue is with a more than 100 basis points higher IPO credit spread than PMTU, issued less than two years ago. As I see things, the underwriters find the senior unsecured debt of PennyMac Mortgage more risky with each new issue, and by definition, the senior unsecured debt quality is the credit quality of the company itself.

Let's take a look at the current pricing of these three baby bonds:

All three are priced at approximately 550 basis points above their corresponding benchmark, or they have higher credit spreads than at their corresponding IPOs, shown above. If these were investment-grade securities, I'd say they were undervalued. This, however, is not the case here by far - these are baby bonds that bear substantial credit risk. My read on the situation is that the market participants are demanding an even higher risk premium than the underwriters to hold these securities in their portfolios.

Sector Comparison

The following section contains baby bonds that pay a fixed interest rate, trading with a par value of $25, issued by a Real Estate Investment Trust with listed common stock. We selected issues with maturity dates of up to 7 years and compared them by yield to maturity. We left the SACH baby bonds out of the comparison, as we believe they should be subject to a different kind of analysis. In my opinion, the case there looks to be beyond repair, illustrating firsthand what can go wrong in the sector.

The metrics of the baby bonds:

The baby bonds of PennyMac Mortgage are among the lowest yielders in the sector. That could indicate a higher degree of trust from the investors in the management team of the company compared to the direct peers, but also offers them the least benefit for taking an exposure to the same sector risk.

Use of Proceeds

We intend to use the net proceeds from this offering to fund our business and investment activities, which may include: the investment in subordinated bonds from our private-label securitization activities and other mortgage-related securities; the acquisition of MSRs; funding our correspondent lending business, including the purchase of Agency-eligible residential mortgage loans; repayment of other indebtedness, which may include the repurchase or repayment of a portion of the 2026 notes or secured financing; and for other general business purposes.

Source: PMTW Prospectus

Special Considerations

Upon a Change of Control Repurchase Event, we will be required to make an offer to repurchase all outstanding notes at a price in cash equal to 101% of the principal amount of the notes, plus accrued and unpaid interest to, but excluding, the repurchase date

Source: PMTW Prospectus

Addition to the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF

The face amount outstanding of the new exchange-traded baby bond PMTW, of $100 million, makes it a potential addition to the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index during some of its upcoming rebalancing. If that happens, it will also be included in the holdings of the main benchmark iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF). PFF is an exchange-traded fund that seeks to track the investment results of the above-mentioned index and is rather important for us due to its influence on the behavior of many of the exchange-traded securities.

Conclusion

This is a short informational article about PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust's new senior note IPO, PMTW. With these articles, we want to pay attention to all new preferred stocks and baby bonds, to evaluate their merits and the potential dangers of investing in them.

PMTW is a smaller copy of PMTV, the earlier this year issued baby bond by PennyMac Mortgage Investment. Despite the same nominal yield, the new IPO has a higher risk premium than its brother, and this does not speak in favor of the company. The security is unrated, but its IPO credit spread suggests it can be placed somewhere below Single-B-rated fixed-rate securities. The high yield it offers the market is associated with a substantial credit risk, so investors' discretion is required. The company is heavily leveraged with a lot of secured debt, a large chunk of which is long-term recourse. All these are considerations that should be taken into account by any potential investor. PMTW is not my investing cup of tea - too risky for my liking. I'm giving it the neutral 'Hold' score.

data.title: Shopify: If You Can't Beat Them, Join Them
data.description:
Shopify (NASDAQ:SHOP) (TSX:SHOP:CA) consistently ranks among the highest-valued companies in public markets, trading at 13 times sales and 75 times forward earnings.

It is the type of company where you need to look far into the future to justify buying at the current price, and many investors choose to just sit on their hands, waiting for a dip.

So, let's look into the future, and search for the right entry point.

Introduction To Shopify

Shopify is a leading 'Retail Operating System' provider, with an expansive suite of offerings that answer virtually every single digital need a seller might have.

From small DTC stores, to medium primarily online businesses like Gymshark, to large enterprises like NIKE (NKE), Skims, and Coach (TPR), Shopify has got its customers covered with everything they need.

Under one umbrella, customers get solutions for payments (on and offline), capital, FX, shipping, international expansion, back office (including tax, CRM, and more), marketing, website building, AI assistants, the list goes on and on.

Judged by Shopify's consistent market share gains across all its areas of business, it is clear that each of its solutions are both best-of-breed, and benefit from their integration to an unparalleled platform.

This is all evidenced by another extraordinary Q1.

Results Continue To Be Almost Unbelievable

Out there in the real world, companies are talking about weak consumer sentiment, tariff uncertainty, and elongated sale cycles. Well, in Shopify's world, there are no signs of slowing down.

GMV rose 23% in Q1, reaching close to $75 billion. Revenue increased 27% to $2.4 billion, reflecting a 10 bps increase in the attach rate. Of note, Shopify achieved this elevated growth rate despite near-term headwinds from moving from a one-month free trial to a three-month free trial, primarily affecting MRR growth. Also, they are starting to lap last year's price increase.

On the profitability front, gross margin is declining as payments penetration rises, having reached 64% in Q1. Payments is margin-dilutive, as Shopify has partnerships with third parties like PayPal (PYPL), but it's a significant revenue driver and value proposition.

Meanwhile, operating margins of 9% were a Q1 record, as operational leverage more than offsets gross margin pressures.

Looking ahead to the second quarter, Shopify expects another quarter of mid-twenties percentage revenue growth, with declining gross margins yet rising operating margin, and stable FCF margins.

Long-Term Outlook Is Undeniably Attractive

My investment thesis in Shopify remains quite simple. I expect that by the end of this decade, e-commerce retail sales will surpass $8 trillion. Within that, I see Shopify growing to a 10% market share, up from 5.6% in 2024, while in the US it is already at 12%, and in Europe, it's at around 6%-7%. This brings us to about $800 billion in GMV by 2030, up from $300 billion in 2024.

Below that, I expect Shopify's attach rate will gradually increase to about 3.5%, up from 3.1% last quarter, as they constantly expand their offerings, increase product adoption, and occasionally implement price increases. This gets us to $29 billion in sales.

Now, here's the real beautiful part. Shopify operates with gross margins of about 50%. Below the gross profit line, there are virtually no marginal costs. Over time, I expect Shopify's operating margin will surpass 20%, up from today's ~12%.

Right now, management is prioritizing market share gains, merchant acquisition, and product development, over near-term margin gains, and that's exactly what they should do. However, over time, operational leverage should be very significant.

I believe that the above assumptions are very reasonable, and I'd be surprised if Shopify doesn't exceed them, quite handily. Nevertheless, based on these assumptions, the company is set to do $6.5 billion in net income and $9.5 billion in free cash flow in 2030, which reflects a 22x and a 14x multiple on today's price.

Place any reasonable exit multiple on that, and you'll get a mid-teens annual return.

Valuation & Near-Term Considerations

At $106 a share, Shopify remains richly valued based on near-term expectations, trading at 75 times forward earnings, and 59 times next year's estimates.

As I wrote in a previous article, when a company trades at such valuation, the only path for investor returns in the near term is if it constantly exceeds expectations. If results begin to erode, the downside risk is significant, as the multiple could contract by 30% or even higher without reaching undeniably attractive levels.

In Shopify's case, I don't see a high probability of missing expectations in Q2, but I do think there's a chance that guidance for Q3 will fall short. Right now, consensus estimates see Shopify doing 24% growth in Q2 (lower than guidance), and 22% in Q3, which is lapping tough comps, and should have no benefit from a price increase.

In addition, there is some chance that macro uncertainty may affect management's confidence in guiding too ambitiously.

Conclusion

By 2030, I see Shopify shares doubling, and over a longer timeframe, I see Shopify surpassing the $500 billion market cap mark.

That said, the current valuation is quite a prohibitive entry point, and if there was ever a setup for some near-term disappointment from Shopify, I think it is now.

Of course, this is all relatively speaking, as I still expect the company to grow revenues at a 20%+ place with expanding margins. It's just that consensus expectations are high, and the stock is at 75 times forward earnings.

As such, I think long-term investors will do well by buying slowly, looking to capitalize on dips. I wouldn't go aggressive here just yet, and would market Q2 earnings as a potential catalyst for a more attractive entry point.

All in all, I reiterate a cautious 'Buy'.

data.title: John B. Sanfilippo & Son's Drop Has Been Nutty (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Even though I hate to see shares of companies fall, the good news is that it opens up the door to jump in when the stock has finally gotten cheap enough. One company that, I feel, is time to upgrade is John B. Sanfilippo & Son (NASDAQ:JBSS), a nut processing and distribution company that focuses on providing cashews, mixed nuts, peanuts, and snack mixes. Back in September of last year, I mentioned that it would be ‘a little nutty’ to upgrade the company. Even though the general picture for the industry was positive from a production standpoint, prices had been on the decline. Furthermore, shares of the company were not cheap enough to justify a bullish assessment in my view.

Since then, the picture has changed. Admittedly, there has been some weakness for the company on its top line. But the most recent bottom-line results show some improvement. The stock has also fallen by 32.5% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 5.1%. This return disparity is certainly disappointing. But given how the stock is priced, it opens up the door for investors who are value oriented like I am to view this opportunity in a more favorable light. Because of this, I have decided to upgrade the company to a soft ‘buy.’

Taking another crack at this prospect

When I last wrote about John B. Sanfilippo & Son last September, investors had access to data covering through the end of the 2024 fiscal year. Results now extend through the third quarter of 2025. During the time since my previous article, the business has seen both positive and negative results. As an example, for the first nine months of the 2025 fiscal year, revenue for the company was $838.2 million. That represented a 5.1% increase over the $797.2 million the business reported one year earlier.

This increase for the company came largely as a result of its consumer distribution channel. Overall revenue jumped 6.7% from $651.7 million to $695.5 million. Management attributed this to a 6.1% increase in volume. But this was not because of increased demand. Instead, it was the company’s acquisition of some snack bar assets in Lakeville, Minnesota. This cost shareholders $61.5 million in cash. But the overall net purchase price was about $59 million. This purchase, which was completed in September of 2023, added 48.5 million pounds to the company's volume in the 2024 fiscal year. Excluding this purchase, total volume for the company for the first nine months of 2025 would have actually declined by 1.4% because of soft consumer demand and decreased seasonal nut and trail mix volume from one of the mass merchandising retailers that it provides its goods for. Overall sales would have increased still under this channel, but only by 0.9%, and that was because of higher pricing.

Even though revenue for the company improved, profitability pulled back. Net income dipped from $50.2 million to $45.4 million. This was in spite of the fact that management was able to decrease costs in some areas. For instance, administrative costs for the company contracted from 4.29% of sales to 3.46%. However, the company saw a rather meaningful reduction in its gross profit margin from 20.59% to 18.46%. It also didn't benefit from the $2.2 million bargain purchase gain that the company reported for the first nine months of the 2024 fiscal year. That was, after all, a one-time event. Regarding the gross profit margin of the company, it suffered because of increased commodity acquisition costs for all of the major nut commodities that it sells, with the exception of pecans. There were also competitive pricing issues and intentional strategic decisions that the company made that impacted results in the near term. Considering that the demand environment for these goods has been soft, these kinds of pressures are not surprising.

Other profitability metrics for the company worsened as well. The biggest problem was operating cash flow, which dropped from $66.4 million to negative $6 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get only a small decline from $73.8 million to $73.5 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company also contracted, dropping from $84.3 million to $82 million. In the grand scheme of things, these declines are not all that bad.

We are seeing some interesting results when it comes to the most recent quarter. This would be the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During this time, revenue did drop from $271.9 million to $260.9 million. This was in spite of the fact that the company saw a nice jump of 13.6% in its contract manufacturing channel, with revenue expanding from $18.9 million to $21.5 million that management attributed to increased granola volume at its new Lakeville facility.

The weakness for the company came from its other two distribution channels. Revenue for the consumer distribution channel dropped 5% year over year because of a 16% plunge in bars volume that management blamed on reduced sales to a mass merchandising retailer after a temporary increase in bar sales caused by a national brand recall that occurred in the third quarter of 2024. The firm was also hit by its decision to reduce sales at a grocery store retailer, as well as from its move to discontinue peanut butter at that same retailer. There were other issues as well, such as an unexplained decline and another grocery retailer. There was also an 8.6% drop in revenue associated with its commercial ingredients district national channel, which management claimed was because of competitive pricing pressures and decreased food service peanut butter sales.

Even though revenue for the company contracted during this time, profitability actually got better. Net income jumped from $13.5 million to $20.2 million. A reduction in administrative expenses from $12.2 million to $9.1 million, and growth in the company's gross profit margin from 18.09% to 21.42%, did the heavy lifting here. The drop in administrative costs was attributable to reduced incentive compensation costs. And the improvement in the company's gross profit margin was largely attributable to inventory valuation adjustments caused by significant increases in commodity input costs that were largely associated with walnuts and pecans. Although operating cash flow dropped from $5.2 million to negative $25.9 million, the adjusted figure for it grew from $21.8 million to $30.2 million. On top of this, EBITDA for the company increased from $24 million to $33.9 million.

If we annualize the results that we have seen so far for the year, we would get net profits of $54.4 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $88.5 million, and EBITDA of $102.8 million. With these estimates, and also with the historical results that the company achieved last year, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are multiples that, quite frankly, are attractive to me. Furthermore, in the table below, I compared John B. Sanfilippo & Son to five similar firms. What I found was that, on a price to earnings basis, only two of the five companies were cheaper than our candidate is. And when it came to the other two profitability metrics, this decreased to only one of the five.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
John B. Sanfilippo & Son 13.4 8.3 8.0
Mission Produce (AVO) 23.0 12.1 9.1
SunOpta (STKL) 9.5 10.1 17.2
The Simply Good Foods Company (SMPL) 23.7 18.7 14.7
Adecoagro S.A. (AGRO) 10.3 2.8 3.7
Calavo Growers (CVGW) 51.3 24.8 16.7

There are some other important things to take into consideration here. Primarily, I am talking about industry market conditions. According to the University of Georgia, while peanut planted acres in the US can be expected to remain steady this year compared to last year, forward contract prices in Georgia specifically are expected to be between $475 per ton and $525 per ton. For context, in Florida, the average price per ton for August of 2024 through February of this year was about $544. And prices seem to have been somewhere in the range of between $530 per ton and $550 per ton for the year.

Even as production remains stable, per capita peanut consumption is dropping. This was because there was a spike during the COVID-19 pandemic, with consumption hitting 7.6 pounds on a per capita basis. But by 2023, it had pulled back to 7.2 pounds. Peanut butter consumption dropped 4.8%, while peanut candy usage has declined by 7.2%. There are also legitimate concerns when it comes to the export space. US peanut exports account for approximately 22% of total US output and are responsible for 14% of the global peanut export market share. And with major destinations like Mexico, Canada, and China, and the trade war situation that's going on there, it stands to reason that there would be uncertainty of the impact from tariffs.

During the first nine months of the 2025 fiscal year, peanuts and peanut butter accounted for only 16.1% of the company's revenue. Most of its sales came from trail and snack mixes, snack and nutrition bars, and certain tree nuts like pecans, cashews, walnuts, and almonds. This is a massive industry, and the US exports a lot of what it produces. In fact, in 2024, the US exported $9.83 billion worth of tree nuts. The largest buyer ended up being the countries in the European Union at $2.72 billion. But India, China, and Canada, are other major purchasers.

For 2025, the picture for the company looks like it will remain weak. For instance, management acknowledged in their latest quarterly earnings call that they are having to contend with higher commodity costs for most nuts that they purchase. This is because of supply and demand volatility. They have resorted to having what they call ‘difficult discussions’ with their customers in order to pass on those price increases that they deem necessary. Of course, they are working with those customers in order to mitigate cost increases, including making changes in pack sizes, product formulas, and product mixes. Tariffs also remain a big issue, especially because some of their goods, like cashews, pine nuts, and macadamias, have to be imported into the country. Add on top of this other concerns like the prospect of the economy weakening, and management definitely views the near term environment in a negative light.

Long term, management is still focused on growing the business, though. During their third quarter earnings call, they mentioned that they plan to invest $90 million in their domestic production capabilities and infrastructure improvements between now and the end of the 2026 fiscal year. This will involve expanding its bar production capacity and making other infrastructure improvements in order to create long-term benefits. This is certainly encouraging and should help boost sales over the next 18 months.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I can understand why some investors might be concerned. I honestly wouldn't be shocked to see the fundamental condition of the company worsen over the next several months or the next year. Having said that, market conditions will eventually stabilize and management's focus on continued growth is promising. Shares look attractively priced at this moment, and I believe that those who are focused on the long haul should view the company in a favorable light.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 06/15/25
data.description:
