data.title: The Raging Conflict Between Israel And Iran, $100 Oil Might Be Possible
data.description:
The last couple of days now have been very interesting for market participants. Major market indices plummeted on June 13th, driven by worries about conflict between Israel and Iran. There has been a lot of rhetoric between the two nations over the last several decades, and there have been instances of fighting before. There are concerns, however, that this time could see the situation elevate to an entirely new level of catastrophe. Any sort of war, especially one that could see the US step in to defend an ally, poses downside risk for the market. Wars are costly, not only in terms of the lives lost, but also in terms of the wealth destroyed in them.

For most aspects of the economy, a widening of this conflict in the Middle East will prove to be a net negative. Off the top of my head, I can only think of two industries that might thrive should conflict get worse from here. The first and most obvious would be the defense industry. And the second would be the oil industry. In fact, even as major market indices declined, concerns about a potential supply disruption pushed crude prices higher. On June 13th alone, WTI crude prices jumped 7.6%, though at one point they were up more than 10%. This war premium, as you can appropriately call it, offers opportunities for oil investors. And it certainly, at least in my opinion, makes things a little less concerning for the industry for this year amidst trade war and broader economic concerns.

A look at the conflict

In what has been called Operation Rising Lion, the Israeli government made a bold decision to use over 200 of its aircraft and drones in order to conduct strikes on key military sites within Iran. This occurred in the early morning hours of June 13th and primarily centered around nuclear facilities. However, it also involved military, missile, and command sites within the nation. This included in the capital city of Tehran, but also involved several other cities as well. We don't know the death toll as of this writing. We may never know. But in all likelihood, hundreds have been killed from the attack.

The death toll included three incredibly powerful individuals in Iran. One of these was Hossein Salami, the head of Iran’s Revolutionary Guards. Another major casualty was Mohammad Bagheri, who served as the chief of staff of the country's armed forces. And then, there was Ali Shamkhani, who was a senior advisor to Ali Khamenei, the Supreme Leader of Iran who has controlled the country since assuming power in 1989.

This outbreak has become increasingly likely in recent years. Under the first Trump Administration, Donald Trump decided to put an end to a deal that had successfully prevented Iran from further developing its nuclear capabilities. Even though the country was in compliance with that deal, he felt as though their decision to continue developing other capabilities and to continue lobbing threats of violence against the US and Israel justified pulling out of the deal and slapping harsh sanctions on the country. Since then, there has been increased concern of the country further developing its nuclear capabilities and eventually taking significant military action against the US and/or Israel. The final straw was likely the fact that the IAEA, which is the nuclear watchdog of the United Nations, lambasted Iran recently for breaching its non-proliferation obligations after the country achieved a 60% enrichment rate for uranium earlier this year. This is the first time that the country has been deemed to be in breach of these obligations in almost 20 years.

Irrespective of your opinion of the situation, there is no denying that this attack by Israel served to escalate things further. I say this because additional attacks, including against other military bases and nuclear sites, as well as at the Mehrabad Airport outside of Tehran, spurred a retaliatory response by Iran against Israel. This included significant drone and ballistic missile launches, some of which have resulted in injuries and fatalities, and many of which were intercepted over not only Israel, but over Jordan, Syria, and Saudi Arabia. There has also been action by the Houthis in Yemen aimed toward Jerusalem.

These events, as well as any further escalation of them, will certainly prove to be bullish for the oil industry. Under the first Trump Administration, tough sanctions on Iran brought oil production down significantly. From 2017 through 2020, daily oil production from the country plummeted by 36.8% from 4.76 million barrels per day to 3.01 million barrels per day. Under the Biden Administration, however, oil production began rising again. This wasn't because sanctions on the country were lifted. Instead, it's because the country got creative when it came to shipping its oil and because of waivers granted to certain countries like China that enabled, on a temporary basis, increased buying of Iranian oil exports. The end result was a return to 4.68 million barrels per day last year.

Any sort of prolonged conflict with China risks causing oil production from that country to drop, perhaps significantly. Already, global oil markets had been in an interesting position. In the chart above, you can see daily production and consumption, on a global scale, for crude oil, as well as the excess supply during these years. Last year, for instance, the world produced around 0.06 million barrels per day more oil than it consumed. On its own, this is a small amount. But over the course of a year, it would total 21.9 million barrels of extra crude oil. For 2025, it was estimated that supply would be 0.82 million barrels per day above what demand called for. That would be an extra 299.3 million barrels of crude oil. And estimates provided by the EIA (Energy Information Administration) called for supply to outpace demand next year by 0.56 million barrels per day, amounting to an extra 204.4 million barrels of oil.

Oil prices are complex. Because the industry is denominated largely in U.S. dollars, changes in the strength of the dollar compared to other currencies can play a role in pricing. Worries about the economy can also affect pricing. But one interesting thing to keep in mind is that, typically, a balanced market for oil is one in which commercial OECD inventories range between 50 and 60 days' worth of supply. When you move outside this range, it's not uncommon to see big changes in pricing.

In the chart below, for instance, you can see how WTI crude pricing changed as commercial OECD inventories, as measured using days of supply, have changed as well. A jump in days of supply between 2013 and 2016 saw a corresponding decline in pricing. 2017 through 2019 saw a drop, with crude pricing rising. When there was a plunge in crude pricing in 2020 because of the COVID-19 pandemic, that was because of a surge in commercial OECD inventories caused by a major reduction in demand. And if we use estimates for this year and next year, we can see how a gradual increase in days of supply corresponds with a decline in pricing.

This disruption from Iran could easily take far more off the market than the gap that exists between global supply and global demand right now. But it's not just because of a potential reduction in crude oil being produced in Iran. It's also because of the possibility of disruption in the region more broadly. This has to do with the Persian Gulf and, within it, the Strait of Hormuz. Dubbed by Reuters in 2023 as ‘the world’s most important oil artery’, the area is responsible for around 20.5 million barrels of crude oil transport every day. This includes not only Iranian crude oil, but also oil from Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Iraq, and the UAE. Qatar is also affected because that country, which serves as the world's largest liquefied natural gas exporter, sends nearly all of its liquefied natural gas through that area.

All told, we are looking at around 20% of all oil used globally each day that flows through that region. And, knowing this, Iran has not been afraid to stir up trouble there. This has included action in most years, including in 2023, 2022, in 2021. Sometimes, that action is minor. But in some cases, like in January 2021, it can be more significant. In that month, the Iranian government seized a South Korean flagged tanker in Gulf waters and detained its crew. And in May 2019, four vessels, including two oil tankers owned by Saudi Arabia, were attacked just outside the Strait of Hormuz. Some of the countries involved can divert some of their oil away from that region. But a lot of it can't be diverted from it. Taking even 1 million barrels per day off of the global market through production decreases in Iran and disruptions at sea could in theory cause a massive jump in pricing. And if total war breaks out, as it is looking increasingly likely, it wouldn't be shocking to see crude oil jump to $100 per barrel or greater.

Takeaway

Right now, this is an interesting time for participants in the oil patch. In almost every respect, war is a bad thing. It costs money. It costs lives, it creates all sorts of issues. But for the oil industry, which has come under increasing pressure in recent months because of worries that the global economy will decline in light of the trade war situation that's going on, this could ultimately be a blessing. I, personally, don't like to bank on geopolitical conflicts. But for those who do like that sort of thing and who are generally bullish about oil, this might be a great time to consider jumping in to some of the players in the space, including oil and gas exploration companies like the ones that I cover at Crude Value Insights.
